issues.description: File::from_raw_fd takes ownership of the fd, so when BufWriter<File> drops at the end of the function, the inner File is dropped, which closes the fd. The code's own comment acknowledges this: 'BufWriter drops here ... But the caller expects the fd to remain open.' This is a use-after-close bug — the caller will attempt to use a closed fd.
issues.suggested_fix: Use File::from_raw_fd followed by std::mem::forget or ManuallyDrop to prevent the File from closing the fd on drop. Alternatively, use BorrowedFd/OwnedFd from std::os::unix::io (stabilized in Rust 1.63+) to express the ownership semantics correctly — BorrowedFd makes it clear the function borrows the fd without taking ownership.

issues.description: The code calls mesh.vertices() and mesh.triangles() as methods, but the actual Mesh struct (reify-types/src/geometry.rs) has public fields: `vertices: Vec<f32>` (flat [x0,y0,z0,...]), `indices: Vec<u32>`, and `normals: Option<Vec<f32>>`. There is no `triangles()` method, and `vertices` is a flat f32 array, not a Vec of structs with .x/.y/.z fields. This code will not compile.
issues.suggested_fix: Use mesh.vertices and mesh.indices directly. Index into the flat vertex array with stride 3: for each index idx, the vertex coords are vertices[idx*3], vertices[idx*3+1], vertices[idx*3+2]. These are already f32 so the `as f32` casts are also unnecessary.

issues.description: The STL binary format writing logic (header, triangle iteration, vertex serialization, attribute bytes) is duplicated between export_stl and export_stl_to_fd. Both write identical binary STL format but to different destinations.
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a shared helper like `fn write_stl_binary(mesh: &Mesh, writer: &mut dyn Write) -> Result<usize>` and call it from both export_stl (wrapping a File) and export_stl_to_fd. This follows the existing codebase pattern where GeometryKernel::export already takes `&mut dyn std::io::Write`.

issues.description: The codebase already has an export architecture: GeometryKernel::export(&self, handle, format, writer: &mut dyn Write). This new function bypasses that trait-based design by implementing STL writing directly, creating a parallel export code path that won't benefit from future kernel-level improvements (e.g., when OCCT STL export is implemented).
issues.suggested_fix: Consider implementing this through the existing GeometryKernel::export trait, or at minimum ensure the shared write helper is the single source of truth for STL binary format, so the kernel can delegate to it when STL support is added.

issues.description: std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd is Unix-only and not used anywhere else in the codebase. This will fail to compile on Windows. The Tauri GUI target may eventually need Windows support.
issues.suggested_fix: If cross-platform support matters, use the Write-trait-based approach (accept &mut dyn Write) and let the caller handle platform-specific fd/handle wrapping. If Unix-only is intentional, add a #[cfg(unix)] gate and document the platform constraint.

opus_strategic.summary: This diff adds an `export_stl_to_fd` function with two blocking issues. First, it takes ownership of the file descriptor via `File::from_raw_fd` but the function's own comment acknowledges the fd will be closed on drop, contradicting the API contract that the caller retains responsibility — this is a use-after-close bug. Second, the code calls non-existent methods (`mesh.vertices()`, `mesh.triangles()`) and treats flat f32 arrays as structs with `.x/.y/.z` fields, meaning it won't compile against the actual `Mesh` type defined in reify-types. Beyond the blockers, the STL binary writing logic is duplicated from the existing `export_stl` function and should be extracted into a shared helper, ideally aligned with the existing `GeometryKernel::export` trait architecture.

issues.description: The function's own comment acknowledges a use-after-close bug: 'BufWriter drops here, which drops the inner File, which closes the fd. But the caller expects the fd to remain open for further operations.' `File::from_raw_fd` takes ownership of the fd and closes it when the `BufWriter<File>` drops at function exit. There is no test exercising this path, so the acknowledged bug has no automated detection. A test using `pipe(2)` or `memfd_create` and attempting to read from the fd after the call returns would immediately catch the double-close / use-after-close.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test: create a pipe with `nix::unistd::pipe()`, call `export_stl_to_fd(mesh, write_fd)`, then attempt to `write` to the write end again or read from the read end. This will surface the premature close. Fix the function by wrapping the fd with `ManuallyDrop<File>` (or `BorrowedFd`) so the file handle is not dropped at end of scope, keeping the underlying fd open for the caller.

issues.description: Neither `export_stl` nor `export_stl_to_fd` has any tests anywhere in the codebase (`grep export_stl crates/reify-runtime` returns nothing). This is an entirely new file introducing two public functions with non-trivial binary-format logic. The `bytes_written` accumulator, the STL header, triangle count encoding, vertex byte layout, and flush are all untested.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a `#[cfg(test)]` module (or a file under `crates/reify-runtime/tests/`) with at minimum: (1) a round-trip test writing to a temp file and re-parsing the STL header + triangle count, (2) a test verifying `bytes_written` equals the file size on disk, (3) a test for `export_stl_to_fd` using an in-process pipe.

issues.description: No test covers a mesh with zero triangles. The function should produce a valid 84-byte STL file (80-byte header + 4-byte zero count) and return `bytes_written == 84`. Without a test, a future refactor could accidentally skip the header for empty meshes.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test: construct a `Mesh` with no vertices and no triangles, call `export_stl_to_fd` into a memfd, assert the fd contains exactly 84 bytes and the triangle-count field reads `0u32`.

issues.description: `bytes_written` is manually maintained with hardcoded increments (`84` for the header, `+50` per triangle) rather than derived from the writer's position. There is no test asserting that the returned value matches `file.metadata().len()` or the number of bytes actually readable from the fd. A mismatch silently gives callers a wrong content-length.
issues.suggested_fix: After flushing, compare `bytes_written` against a seek-based size or the pipe-read byte count in a test. Alternatively, use a `CountingWriter` wrapper to derive the value mechanically.

issues.description: No test verifies the binary layout is a valid STL: that bytes 80–83 encode the triangle count as little-endian u32, that each 50-byte triangle record has the 12-byte normal first, then three 12-byte vertex blocks, then a 2-byte attribute field. An off-by-one in field ordering (e.g., vertices before normal) would go undetected.
issues.suggested_fix: Write a test that exports a single known triangle (e.g., vertices at (0,0,0), (1,0,0), (0,1,0)), then reads back the raw bytes and asserts the exact layout: header 80 bytes, count == 1, normal == [0f32;3], vertex bytes match known values, attribute == 0u16.

issues.description: There is no test for what happens when `fd` is -1 or already closed. `File::from_raw_fd(-1)` is immediate UB on write; the function should return an `Err` rather than panic. Currently unchecked.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test passing `fd = -1` and asserting that `export_stl_to_fd` returns `Err(_)` rather than panicking or invoking UB. Consider validating the fd before calling `from_raw_fd` using `fcntl(fd, F_GETFD)` as a guard.

sonnet_test_analyst.summary: The diff introduces a new `mesh_export.rs` file with two public functions (`export_stl` and `export_stl_to_fd`), but neither function has any tests in the repository. The most critical finding is that `export_stl_to_fd` contains a use-after-close bug that is explicitly acknowledged in a code comment ("BufWriter drops here, which drops the inner File, which closes the fd. But the caller expects the fd to remain open") — yet there is no test that would detect it. `File::from_raw_fd` takes ownership of the fd and closes it on drop, making the function unsafe to call as documented. A basic pipe-based test would immediately surface this. Beyond that, the STL binary format is never validated (header structure, triangle count encoding, vertex layout), the `bytes_written` return value is never compared against actual output size, and boundary conditions (empty mesh, invalid fd) are unexercised. Both blocking issues require tests before this code ships.

issues.description: `File::from_raw_fd(fd)` takes ownership of the fd and will close it when the `File` (and wrapping `BufWriter`) is dropped. Rust drops all locals before returning, so the fd is closed on EVERY exit path — both the success path after `writer.flush()?` and every early-return `?` in the loop. The docstring contradicts this entirely: it says 'The caller is responsible for closing the fd when done' and 'Returns the raw fd that the caller can pass to a socket send operation', but in practice the fd is already closed by the time the function returns `Ok(bytes_written)`. Any caller that subsequently uses or closes the fd will get a use-after-close bug. If another fd is opened between the implicit close and the caller's explicit close, that new fd gets closed instead — a classic double-close security vulnerability.
issues.suggested_fix: Wrap the file in `std::mem::ManuallyDrop` to prevent the drop from running: `let file = ManuallyDrop::new(unsafe { File::from_raw_fd(fd) });`. Then call `BufWriter::new(ManuallyDrop::into_inner(file))` only after you're done, or — better — write directly via `FileDescriptor` / `BorrowedFd` using `std::os::unix::io::BorrowedFd` and a `Write` impl that does not take ownership. Alternatively, restructure so the function takes a `&mut impl Write` instead of a raw fd, pushing lifetime concerns to the caller.

issues.description: `tri_count = (triangles.len() / 3) as u32` uses integer division (truncates toward zero), but `chunks(3)` will also yield a final short chunk when `triangles.len() % 3 != 0`. This means the STL binary header encodes N triangles while the body contains N+1 records (the last being a partial triangle with fewer than 3 vertices). The result is a structurally corrupt STL file that most parsers will reject or misinterpret. There is no assertion or early return to catch a malformed triangle buffer.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a guard at the top of the function: `anyhow::ensure!(triangles.len() % 3 == 0, "triangle buffer length {} is not a multiple of 3", triangles.len());`. Use `triangles.chunks_exact(3)` instead of `chunks(3)` so the compiler helps enforce the invariant and the remainder is surfaced explicitly.

issues.description: `use std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd` is unconditionally imported, making the module fail to compile on non-Unix targets (Windows). The existing `export_stl` function is platform-agnostic; adding a Unix-only function without a cfg gate silently breaks cross-platform builds.
issues.suggested_fix: Gate the entire function and its import with `#[cfg(unix)]` (or `#[cfg(target_family = "unix")]`). Alternatively, accept a `Box<dyn Write>` or `&mut impl Write` instead of a raw fd to remain cross-platform.

issues.description: `bytes_written` is incremented unconditionally by 50 per triangle regardless of whether `writer.flush()` succeeds. Because `BufWriter` buffers writes, `bytes_written` counts bytes handed to the buffer, not bytes actually written to the fd. If `flush()` fails mid-buffer, the returned count will overstate the number of bytes the caller's peer actually received.
issues.suggested_fix: Either remove `bytes_written` tracking (the size is deterministic: `84 + 50 * tri_count as usize`) and compute it directly, or only return the count after a successful flush, which the current code already does on the happy path — but document clearly that the count reflects what was buffered, not what was flushed.

issues.description: The doc comment says 'Returns the raw fd that the caller can pass to a socket send operation' but the return type is `Result<usize>` (bytes written). The claim about fd ownership in the doc is also incorrect (see the blocking issue above).
issues.suggested_fix: Correct the doc comment to state what the function actually returns (`usize` = bytes written to the stream) and remove or correct the claim about fd lifetime once the ownership model is fixed.

sonnet_robustness.summary: This diff introduces `export_stl_to_fd` which has one critical correctness bug and one critical data-integrity bug. The most severe issue is that `File::from_raw_fd` takes ownership of the provided fd, so Rust's drop glue closes the fd before the function returns — on every code path, success or failure. The docstring falsely promises the caller can still use or close the fd afterward; any attempt to do so is use-after-close, and a double-close is a security vulnerability. The fix is to use `ManuallyDrop<File>` or accept a `&mut impl Write` instead. The second blocking issue is that `tri_count` is computed with truncating integer division while `chunks(3)` yields a remainder chunk when the buffer length is not divisible by 3, producing a structurally corrupt STL file; `chunks_exact(3)` plus an upfront `ensure!` guard is the correct fix. Two additional suggestions cover the unconditional Unix-only import (portability) and a misleading doc comment.
